title: Isolation levels and the anomalies they allow
description: Read-committed allows non-repeatable reads and lost updates; serializable prevents anomalies but can abort.
body: Use when a transaction's correctness depends on concurrent behavior. Postgres defaults to read-committed, where each statement sees a fresh snapshot, so non-repeatable reads and read-modify-write lost updates are possible. Repeatable-read gives a stable snapshot for the whole transaction (no non-repeatable reads, blocks some anomalies) but can fail with a serialization error on conflicting updates. Serializable adds predicate tracking to prevent write skew, at the cost of more serialization-failure aborts the app must retry. Do not assume the default protects a read-modify-write. Actions: use FOR UPDATE or raise the level and add retry logic. Validation signals include a concurrency test producing correct results. Risk boundary: a money/inventory update without locking or sufficient isolation can silently lose writes.
